# Writing Scripts

{{< video https://vimeo.com/1198721981/ebd1da6e9f?fl=ml&fe=ec >}}

We write our first R script, learn how to run code line by line and in bulk, and discuss the difference between the console and a saved script file. This is the code that we saved in our script:


```{r filename="01-getting-started.R", eval = FALSE}
# My first R script
# SKL402 - Getting started

# Some basic arithmetic
2 + 3
10 / 3
sqrt(16)
```

## Exercises

### Exercise 1.4.1

Create a new script file called `exercises_topic1.R` in your `scripts/` folder. At the top, write a comment with your name and today's date. Save the file.

<details> <summary>Solution</summary>

```r
# exercises_topic1.R
# Author: [Your name]
# Date:   [Today's date]
# Topic 1 exercises for SKL400
```

Save as `scripts/exercises_topic1.R`.

</details>

### Exercise 1.4.2

In your script, write code that computes and prints the result of:

1. 17 modulo 5 (the remainder when 17 is divided by 5)
2. Integer division of 17 by 5

Run the lines from the script. (_Hint_: the modulo operator in R is `%%` and integer division is `%/%`.)

<details> <summary>Solution</summary>

```r
# 1. Modulo
17 %% 5
```

```
[1] 2
```

```r
# 2. Integer division
17 %/% 5
```

```
[1] 3
```

Check: 5 × 3 + 2 = 17. ✓

</details>

### Exercise 1.4.3

Write a comment in your script explaining what the following line of code does, then run it:

```r
ceiling(7.3)
```

<details> <summary>Solution</summary>

```r
# ceiling() rounds a number UP to the nearest integer
ceiling(7.3)
```

```
[1] 8
```

Related functions: `floor()` rounds down, `round()` rounds to the nearest integer (or specified number of decimal places).

</details>

### Exercise 1.4.4

Experiment with the keyboard shortcuts in Positron. Write three lines of code in your script:

```r
10 + 5
10 - 5
10 * 5
```

Run them one at a time using the keyboard shortcut (Ctrl+Enter or Cmd+Enter). Then select all three lines and run them as a block. What is the difference in how the output appears?

<details> <summary>Solution</summary>

Running one at a time: each line is sent to the console individually, and the result appears after each line.

Running as a block: all three lines are sent together, and you see them executed in sequence:

```
> 10 + 5
[1] 15
> 10 - 5
[1] 5
> 10 * 5
[1] 50
```

The results are the same, but running line by line gives you more control for checking intermediate results.

</details>

### Exercise 1.4.5

On purpose, write a line of code that will produce an error, for example:

```r
5 + "hello"
```

Run it. Read the error message. Can you understand what it is telling you? Fix the line so it runs without error (for example, change it to `5 + 3`).

<details> <summary>Solution</summary>

```r
5 + "hello"
```

```
Error in 5 + "hello" : non-numeric argument to binary operator
```

The error tells us that the `+` operator (a "binary operator" because it takes two arguments) received a non-numeric argument (`"hello"` is a character string). R cannot add a number and a string.

Fix:

```r
5 + 3
```

```
[1] 8
```

</details>
